title: Branching laws: homological aspects
speaker: Dipendra Prasad
abstract: This lecture will partly survey branching laws for real and p-adic groups which often is related to period integrals of automorphic representations, discuss some of the more recent developments, focusing attention on homological aspects and the Bernstein decomposition.

proof_stages.label: Local branching problem and multiplicity-one baseline
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins with local restriction problems for irreducible admissible representations, motivated by automorphic periods, and recalls multiplicity-one and nonvanishing questions for Hom spaces such as SO(n+1) restricted to SO(n).

proof_stages.label: Ext and Euler-Poincare formalism for branching
proof_stages.summary: The Hom problem is enlarged to Ext groups and their Euler-Poincare alternating sum, with finiteness and vanishing results, projective/injective tools, Frobenius reciprocity for Ext, and qualitative expectations about higher Ext in both restriction directions.

proof_stages.label: GL(n+1) to GL(n) model calculation
proof_stages.summary: For GL(n+1) restricted to GL(n), the generic Hom theorem is refined to an Euler-Poincare formula in terms of Whittaker dimensions, proved via the Bernstein-Zelevinsky mirabolic filtration and derivatives, then supplemented by higher-Ext vanishing/projectivity and Bernstein-block refinements.

proof_stages.label: Bessel-model reduction for classical groups
proof_stages.summary: For orthogonal groups SO(V) to SO(W), Bessel subgroups and Bessel models replace the mirabolic apparatus and provide explicit Euler-Poincare calculations in principal-series cases.

proof_stages.label: Waldspurger integral as conjectural Euler-Poincare formula
proof_stages.summary: Waldspurger's character-germ integral over selected elliptic tori is reviewed as a formula for Hom dimensions in tempered or supercuspidal cases and then proposed as the Euler-Poincare pairing for finite-length representations, with spherical-variety generalizations in view.

proof_stages.label: Schneider-Stuhler duality and submodule consequences
proof_stages.summary: Schneider-Stuhler duality is invoked to relate Ext groups in opposite directions through the dualizing representation, turning quotient-style branching questions into submodule questions and yielding concrete classifications such as the GL2 triple-product case.

proof_stages.label: Algebraic-geometric dictionary for Ext phenomena
proof_stages.summary: The speaker organizes the preceding results by analogy with coherent sheaf cohomology on projective varieties: finite-dimensionality, vanishing, semicontinuity, Riemann-Roch, Kodaira-type generic vanishing, Serre duality, and the special role of projective space versus GL_n.

proof_stages.label: Geometrization of Ext classes by equivariant spaces
proof_stages.summary: The final program proposes constructing representation-theoretic Ext classes from equivariant sheaves on G-spaces and exact sequences arising from proper maps or open-closed decompositions, with Bruhat-Tits building compactifications as evidence and non-splitting as a remaining challenge.

examples.name: Automorphic period integrals
examples.note: The period integral ∫_H f(h) dh is used as a motivating global source of local branching Hom spaces and their relation to L-values, not as part of the homological argument.

examples.name: GL3/GL2 quotient pairs with nonzero higher Ext
examples.note: Pairs where a generic GL3 representation has the trivial GL2 representation as a quotient, or a nongeneric GL3 representation has a generic GL2 quotient, illustrate that EP can vanish while Hom is nonzero, forcing higher Ext.
examples.engagement_reason: He applies the GL(n+1)/GL(n) Euler–Poincaré formula to the GL3/GL2 quotient pairs to infer EP is zero while Hom is nonzero, hence some higher Ext must be nonzero.

passes.reason: The transcript includes a trivial GL2 quotient and uses the pairs to show higher Ext-vanishing cannot hold generally.
The pairs involve a nongeneric/trivial representation, and EP = 0 together with Hom nonzero forces nonzero higher Ext, blocking any unrestricted Ext-vanishing claim.
The GL3/GL2 pairs involve trivial or nongeneric constituents and show higher Ext need not vanish without genericity.
The GL3/GL2 pairs involve a nongeneric or trivial member and are used to force nonzero higher Ext despite EP being zero, refuting blanket vanishing.
The GL3/GL2 quotient pairs are ordinary members of the GLn setting but are used to show higher Ext groups need not vanish.
The GL3/GL2 quotient pairs are used to show higher Ext need not vanish once genericity hypotheses are dropped.
The GL3/GL2 quotient pairs include a trivial or nongeneric constituent and are used to show that higher Ext vanishing cannot hold without genericity hypotheses.
The nongeneric/trivial quotient pairs force Hom nonzero while EP is zero, refuting blanket higher-Ext vanishing.
Used to show higher-Ext vanishing cannot hold without genericity, since a nongeneric/trivial member gives Hom nonzero but EP zero.

examples.name: Steinberg restriction and absence of submodules
examples.note: The Steinberg/discrete-series restriction to GL_n is used to illustrate projectivity and the quotient-versus-submodule asymmetry later explained by Schneider-Stuhler duality.
examples.engagement_reason: He uses projectivity and Schneider–Stuhler duality for the Steinberg restriction to derive vanishing of higher Exts and absence of irreducible submodules.

passes.reason: The Steinberg restriction is used to show the expected submodule analogue fails despite many quotients.

examples.name: Split Borel-induced case giving EP equal to one
examples.note: When a split orthogonal representation is induced from a Borel character, its character contributes only at the identity elliptic element, making the conjectural integral reduce to EP = 1.
examples.engagement_reason: He analyzes the split Borel-induced case in the integral formula, using support on split tori to reduce the elliptic contribution to the identity and conclude EP equals one.

passes.reason: Induction from a split Borel is a minimal-parabolic/maximally split case giving a positive EP computation.

examples.name: GL2 triple-product submodules
examples.note: The tensor product of two GL2 representations with trivial product central character is used as a concrete submodule problem where supercuspidal quotients reappear as submodules and one special Steinberg-twist case also occurs.

examples.name: Compactified PGL2 Bruhat-Tits tree
examples.note: The compactified zero-skeleton of the Bruhat-Tits tree, with boundary P^1(F), produces an exact sequence and Ext class, and the speaker explicitly uses it to motivate the broader hope that extensions come from equivariant geometric gluings.
examples.engagement_reason: He describes the compactified PGL2 Bruhat–Tits tree, its boundary and orbits, extends a sheaf over it, and uses the resulting exact sequence to construct an Ext class.

examples.engagement_reason: In the discussion he traces the GL2 principal-series family through the reducibility point, identifying the trivial and Steinberg components as where jumping occurs.
